title: Chapter 26: The Garden
content: I shift.

But not to a timeline. Not to a world I know. Not to New York or Paris or Clonakilty or any of the places the app has flung me across the past eleven months like a stone across water, skipping and skipping and skipping until I sink.
I'm standing in a garden.
Not a real garden. An impossible one.

The hedges rise on every side — green, impossibly green, the kind of green that doesn't exist in nature, that exists only in the word "green" itself, the platonic ideal of green. They stretch in every direction, neat rows of them, each one trimmed to a precision that would make a French groundskeeper weep. And they fork. Every path splits into two, then each of those splits into two more, and two more, and two more, until the hedges are a combinatorial explosion made visible, a fractal rendered in boxwood and shadow, extending infinitely in every direction.

I look up. The sky is white. Not cloudy white. Not overcast white. A flat, featureless, infinite white, like a canvas that's never been touched, like a screen waiting for an image that will never come.
The air hits me and I nearly stagger.
It smells like everything at once.

Spring — tulips and wet soil and that electric green smell of things just starting to grow. Summer — cut grass and hot asphalt and the sweetness of fruit rotting in the sun. Autumn — woodsmoke and dry leaves and the faint caramel of things breaking down. Winter — frost and pine and the metallic nothing of cold so deep it burns.

All of it. Simultaneously. My lungs can't parse it. My brain can't categorize it. Every season is happening to my body at the same time and my eyes water and my skin prickles and I know — I *know* — what this is.

I read about this in a college dorm room at two in the morning, fueled by cheap coffee and the desperate conviction that I was the kind of person who read Borges for fun. I wasn't. I was the kind of person who wanted to be the kind of person who read Borges for fun. But the story stayed with me anyway, lodged under my ribs like a splinter that never worked its way out.
The Garden of Forking Paths.

A garden where every possible future exists simultaneously. Where every choice spawns a new path, and every path is real, and none of them cancel each other out. A garden where time is not a line but a web, not a river but an ocean, not a story with one ending but a story with every ending.
It was fiction then.
It is not fiction now.
I start walking.
What else am I going to do? Stand here and wait for the white sky to answer me? I've been waiting for answers my whole life. I'm done waiting.

The gravel crunches beneath my shoes — or maybe it doesn't. Maybe the sound exists the way the smells exist, as a possibility rather than a fact. The paths branch and rebranch and I take them at random, left, right, left, left, right, and at every intersection I see things through the hedges.
Glimpses. Fragments. Lives.

Through one gap: the glass towers of Sterling Capital, rising into a sky I almost recognize, and there's a figure at a desk — me? Is that me? — wearing a blazer I owned once, typing something, a coffee cup at her elbow, and she looks purposeful, she looks like she knows exactly what she's doing, she looks like a person who has never once wondered if she's in the wrong life.

Through another: a flash of the Paris apartment, the one on Rue des Beaux-Arts with the windows that don't quite close and the neighbor who plays Debussy at three in the morning, and I can see the cream walls and the copper pots and the light — God, the light — that particular Paris light that makes everything look like a painting of itself.

Through a third: the sound of Irish rain. Not the sight of it, just the sound, a steady drumming on a tin roof, and underneath it the smell of peat smoke and the sea, and I can almost feel the weight of a wool blanket on my shoulders, the warmth of a body beside mine on a couch that's too small for two people but we make it work because we always make it work—
But there are other paths too. Paths I didn't take.

One where I stayed in New York and never downloaded the app. Where I'm still at Sterling Capital, still reporting to Marcus, still running projections for deals I don't care about, still dating men who look good on paper and feel like cardboard in my arms. That Avery is fine. She's okay. She's surviving. She's also not quite alive.

Another where I chose London instead of Paris. A flat in Notting Hill. A job at a fintech startup that pays in equity and free beer. A Julian with a British accent who says "brilliant" too much and makes me laugh but doesn't make me — what? Doesn't make me *stop*. Doesn't make me hold my breath. Doesn't make me feel like the axis of the world has shifted three degrees and everything is the same but everything is different.

And another where I'm alone in all of them. Where I chose myself. Where the app is deleted and the timelines are someone else's problem and I'm in a city I picked for myself, living a life I designed, and I'm fine, I'm good, I'm—
I'm alone.
Every possible Avery. Every possible choice. All visible. All adjacent. All unreachable.
I walk faster.

The paths are narrowing now, or maybe I'm just getting better at choosing between them, and the glimpses through the hedges come faster and blurrier, a montage of every life I could have lived cut to the beat of my own quickening pulse, and then—
The paths stop.
They converge.

All of them. Every branching, forking, splitting path I've been walking through — they all end up here. In a small clearing, circular, perfectly round, like the center of a target. Like the eye of a storm. Like the point where every line of every geometry meets and says *this* — this is the place. This is where it was always going.
There's a stone bench at the center.
He's sitting on it.

I know it's him before I can articulate why. The shape of him is Julian — the height, the way he holds his shoulders, the particular angle of his head when he's about to say something that matters. But his face.
His face is all three faces at once.

Julian A's sharp cheekbones, the ones that catch light like the edges of glass. Julian B's warm eyes, brown as French roast coffee, the ones that crinkle at the corners when he smiles. Julian C's scar, the thin white line that traces from his left temple to his jaw, the one he got when he was fourteen and thought he could ride a horse bareback because his cousins dared him.
All three. Superimposed. Layered like a palimpsest, or a long-exposure photograph, or a face seen through a prism that splits one man into a spectrum.

He's wearing clothes that don't match. Half a dark suit jacket over a wrinkled linen shirt over faded denim, and it should look absurd, it should look like a costume party disaster, but it doesn't. It looks like a man assembled from love letters written in three different languages. Each piece fits the way poetry fits — not logically, not structurally, but emotionally, in the place where meaning lives before it becomes words.
He looks up at me.
"You found me," he says.

And his voice is layered. Three voices, speaking in harmony, not unison. Julian A's precision, every consonant sharp as a scalpel. Julian B's warmth, vowels rounded and soft as river stones. Julian C's rawness, a roughness at the edges like a song recorded in a garage, all heart, no polish.

They say my name the same way they say everything else — three times at once. Avery. Avery. Avery. Three different inflections, three different weights, three different ways of loving the same syllable, and each one hits me separately, punch punch punch, and then they combine into a sound that has no single equivalent in any world, a sound that's just — him. All of him.
"Or I found you," he says, the harmony shifting, the voices rearranging themselves. "Or we found each other. It's hard to tell in here."
He gestures at the garden. At the impossible hedges. At the white sky. At the billion paths that all led to this bench, this clearing, this moment.
"This is where the paths meet," he says. "Where all the choices converge. I've been waiting for you."
My legs are shaking. I don't know if it's the garden or the grief or the gravity of all those collapsing timelines pulling at me. I cross the clearing and I sit next to him on the bench.

It's warm. The stone is warm, like it's been sitting in sunlight, even though there's no sun. Like it's been waiting for someone to rest. Like the garden built this bench specifically for this moment — two people at the center of everything, finally still.

The garden hums. Not with sound, exactly. With energy. With the accumulated voltage of a billion possible lives, all pressing against the edges of the hedges, all vibrating at the frequency of *what if*. It hums in my teeth, in my bones, in the marrow of me.
"What are you?" I ask.

Because I have to. Because he's not Julian A or Julian B or Julian C and he's also all of them and I need to understand what I'm looking at before it destroys me, before I destroy it, before whatever's coming comes.

He considers. I can see him considering — three minds working in parallel, three thought processes converging on a single answer, and the effort it takes, the way his brow furrows in three slightly different patterns, it almost breaks me.
"I'm what happens when three versions of the same soul love the same woman so much that the walls between worlds can't hold anymore."
He says it simply. Like it's obvious. Like it's the kind of thing that happens every day, the weather forecast for the end of the multiverse: partly cloudy with a chance of ontological collapse.
"I'm not new," he says. "I'm just... all of them. At once."

He turns to face me and his eyes — three pairs of eyes that are somehow one pair, layered like everything else about him, but looking at me with a steadiness that belongs to all three Julians equally — find mine and hold.
"And I'm dying."
The words land and I feel them in my sternum.
"Because you can only choose one." His voice cracks on the harmony, the three tones briefly dissonant, briefly painful. "And the other two — the other two are me, too."
There it is.

The thesis. The thing I've been running from since the first shift, since the first glitch, since the first time I looked at a man who loved me in one world and realized he loved me in another and another and I couldn't keep them all and I couldn't let any of them go.
To choose is to lose.

Not to lose a timeline. Not to lose a city or a life or a set of coordinates. To lose *him*. To lose versions of him that love me, that are real, that have held me and kissed me and said my name like it was the only word that mattered. To lose them not because they were taken from me but because I *chose* to let them go.
I can't breathe. The mixed-season air catches in my throat and I can't breathe and the garden is humming louder now, the hum becoming a drone, the drone becoming a—
The ground shakes.
The hedges crack.

Not metaphorically — I can hear them, the sound of wood splitting, of neat green walls fracturing into splinters. I can see paths collapsing, the gravel crumbling into nothing, the gaps through which I glimpsed my other lives sealing shut like wounds healing too fast, leaving scars where possibility used to be.
The clock is synchronizing.

I don't need the app to tell me. I can feel it in my bones, in the part of me that's been glitching between worlds long enough to recognize the countdown. Something is aligning. Something is closing. The window between timelines is narrowing to a point, and when it closes, whatever's on the wrong side of the glass — that's it. That's gone. Permanently. Irrevocably. The kind of gone that doesn't come back.
The Composite Julian reaches for my hand.

His grip is warm — all three of them warm, in different ways, the dry certainty of a handshake, the easy casualness of fingers interlocking, the rough desperation of holding on like you're afraid the person will float away.
"Avery." The three voices, urgent now, the harmony tightening. "Listen to me. You have to choose."
"I can't—"

"You have to choose. Not because the app tells you to. Not because the clock is running out. Not because the garden is collapsing." His grip tightens. "Because every moment you don't, a piece of me dies, and a piece of you dies with it."

His face — all three faces — so close to mine. I can see the layers. I can see the cheekbones, the eyes, the scar. I can see every Julian I've loved superimposed like an emotional double exposure, and it's beautiful and it's unbearable and it's about to be impossible.
"But don't choose the timeline," he says.
I blink. "What?"

"Don't choose the timeline," he repeats, and now the voices are almost chanting, a Gregorian harmony of advice, the most important thing any version of him has ever said to me. "Don't choose Paris because of the light. Don't choose New York because of the ambition. Don't choose Clonakilty because of the peace. Those are places. Those are circumstances. Those are the set dressing."
His grip is almost painful now. The garden is collapsing around us. Hedges falling into void. Paths erased mid-step. The white sky cracking like an egg, like a screen, like a heart.
"Choose the person. Choose the world where you're most yourself. Because that's where I'll be most myself, too."

The bench lurches. I grab his arm and he grabs mine and for one second — one perfect, impossible, impossibly brief second — I'm holding all three of them at once. I'm holding the sharp one and the warm one and the raw one. I'm holding the precision and the patience and the passion. I'm holding every Julian in every timeline in every possible configuration of the same soul, and he's holding me back, and I can feel the love — three love-sources converging into a single blinding point — and it's everything.
And then it's gone.
The garden shatters.
I'm falling.

White space. Cold white space, the kind of cold that isn't temperature but absence, the cold of nothing around you, nothing below you, nothing to hold onto. The mixed-season smell is gone. The warm bench is gone. The green of the hedges is gone. There's only white and cold and the sensation of falling through a place that isn't a place.
And in the void, I see three faces.

Julian A. Julian B. Julian C. All his. All mine. All ours. They're receding — each one pulling away in a different direction, like galaxies expanding from a shared origin point, and I'm the origin point, I'm the center, I'm the place they're all leaving.
One truth remains, carved into the white space like a scar:
She has to let two of them go.
My phone vibrates. I shouldn't have my phone. There shouldn't be service in the space between worlds. But the app has never cared about what should be. The screen glows against the void:

**INTEGRITY: 3%**
**CHOOSE NOW.**
I'm falling.
I have seconds left.

And I don't know which world I'm most myself in. I don't know which person I'm most myself with. I don't know how to choose without losing, how to hold without breaking, how to love one without killing two.
But I'm running out of time.
And the faces are fading.
And the white space is closing in.
And I have to choose now.
